저.note: Formal/polite 'I'. Use with elders and strangers. Casual form: 나 (Unit 10).

나.note: Casual 'I'. Only use with close friends, younger people, or kids. Never with elders.

우리.note: Koreans say 우리 엄마 (our mom) where English says 'my mom'. Reflects group-first culture.

제.note: Polite possessive. 제 = 저의 contracted. Use with elders and strangers.

이것.note: 것 is one of the most frequent Korean words. 이것/그것/저것 = 3-level distance system.

그것.note: 그 = near the listener (2nd person). English collapses 이/그/저 into just 'this/that'.

저것.note: 저 = far from both speaker and listener. The 3rd level English lacks.

있어요.base: there is / I have / is (at location)

uses.e: The teacher is in the classroom.

있어요.note: 있다 does the job of 3 English verbs: 'there is', 'I have', 'someone is somewhere'.

없어요.base: there is not / I don't have / is not (at location)
없어요.note: Exact inverse of 있어요. Pair them in memory: 있다 ↔ 없다.

있다.note: Dictionary form. Powers 3 English meanings: 'there is', 'I have', 'is somewhere'.

없다.base: to not exist / to not have (dictionary form)
없다.note: Exact inverse of 있다. Irregular stem 없 is indivisible. Builds: 재미없다, 맛없다.

이에요.note: Use after a noun ending in a CONSONANT. 'Copula' = the verb to-be. The verb ALWAYS goes last.

예요.morph: 이에요 contracted when stem ends in vowel
예요.note: Same meaning as 이에요 — use after nouns ending in a VOWEL. The -요 is always the polite marker.

아니에요.note: The subject before 아니에요 takes 이/가 (not 은/는). 아니요 (standalone 'no') is different.

해요.note: 하다 + 어요 contracts to 해요. Most -하다 verbs conjugate this way.

가요.note: 가다 stem is 가 (pure vowel). Add 요 directly → 가요. One of the most used Korean verbs.

와요.note: 오 + 아요 contracts to 와요. Irregular-looking but follows the vowel harmony rule.

먹어요.note: 먹 stem ends in dark vowel ㅓ → takes -어요. One of the highest-frequency Korean verbs.

마셔요.note: 마시 + 어 contracts to 마셔. 이+어 → 여 is a standard Korean contraction.

알아요.note: 알다 is ㄹ-irregular in some forms but 알아요 is regular. Pair with 몰라요 (don't know).

몰라요.note: ⚡ JRTF: 모르다 → 몰라요, NOT 모르어요. The stem transforms. Full pattern in Unit 13.

주세요.note: 주다 → 주세요 = 'please give'. The most useful single request phrase in Korean.

uses.e: This is good. / I like this.

좋아요.note: 좋다 means BOTH 'to be good' and 'to like'. 좋아요 = 'is good' and '(I) like (it)'.

싫어요.note: Stronger than 안 좋아요 (not good). More emotional/direct expression of dislike.

uses.e: Please speak in the polite style.

해요체.note: The 7 Korean speech levels. 해요체 is the safe default for ALL adult interactions.

안.note: 안 ↔ 밖 (outside). DIFFERENT from 안 (negation). Context tells you which 안 it is.

못.note: 못 = physical/circumstantial inability. 안 = won't. The distinction matters in Korean.

지않아요.note: More formal/emphatic than 안. Used in writing, formal speech. Same meaning as 안 + verb.

지못해요.note: Formal version of 못 + verb. 수영을 못해요 = 수영하지 못해요. Same meaning, different register.

uses.e: As for me, I'm a student.

은.note: Sets the topic. Implies contrast: '저는 (not you)'. After vowels → 는.

는.note: Same as 은 but after vowel-ending words. Sets topic, shows contrast, marks known info.

이.note: Marks the doer or new info. Contrast with 은/는 (topic). After vowels → 가.

가.base: ① subject particle  ② verb stem (가다 = to go)
가.morph: 가 is genuinely two things — context determines which

uses.e: ① There is coffee. — 가 = subject particle
② I go to school. — 가 = stem of 가다 (to go)
① What do you like? — 가 = subject particle

가.note: This ambiguity is REAL Korean. When 가 follows a noun → particle. When 가 precedes 요/서/면/고 → verb stem of 가다 (to go). Korean speakers feel the difference instantly — you will too.

uses.e: I swim. (수영 ends in ㅇ = consonant)

을.note: Marks what the verb acts on. Often dropped in casual speech. After vowels → 를.

를.note: Same as 을 but after vowel-ending words. Check the LAST syllable's final consonant.

에.base: to / at / in (location or destination or time)
에.note: 에 marks WHERE something IS, WHERE you're GOING, and WHEN. Does NOT mark where you DO things → 에서.

에서.note: 에서 = where you DO things. Test: is there an action verb? Yes → 에서. Also means 'from'.

도.note: 도 REPLACES 은/는 or 이/가 — it never stacks. 저는도 ✗ / 저도 ✓

의.note: 의 is often DROPPED in casual speech: 엄마의 이름 → 엄마 이름 (same meaning, more natural).

로.note: (으)로 = by means of / in the direction of. 으로 after consonant (except ㄹ), 로 after vowel or ㄹ.

밥.note: 밥 = literally cooked rice, but culturally = any meal. 밥 먹었어요? is how Koreans say 'How are you?'

물.note: 물 ends in consonant ㄹ → 물을 (object marker). Don't let the ㄹ fool you.

커피.note: 커피 = Konglish pronunciation of 'coffee'. Korea has one of the world's highest café densities.

학교.note: 학 reappears in: 대학교 (university), 학원 (academy), 학생 (student).

집.note: 우리 집 (my house) not 나의 집. 집 = home as a concept, not just a building.

학생.note: 학 = study, 생 = life/person. 학생들 = students (들 = plural marker, optional in Korean).

선생님.note: 님 = honorific suffix showing respect. 선생 alone is informal. 선생님 is the safe form.

이름.note: 이름이 뭐예요? = Survival question #1. Koreans often introduce by family name first.

뭐.note: 뭐 is the casual spoken form of 무엇. Used in ~90% of daily questions. One of the top-10 Korean words.

어디.note: 어디에 = at where (static) / 어디에서 = from where or doing where. Critical for directions.

왜.note: 왜요 alone is a complete, natural question. Can sound confrontational — tone matters.

언제.note: Time question. Pairs with 에: 언제에 → 언제 (에 is usually dropped with question words).

얼마나.base: how much / how many / how long
얼마나.note: 얼마 alone = how much money? 얼마나 = to what degree/extent (for adjectives and verbs).

uses.e: How much is it in total?

얼마예요.note: Survival shopping phrase. 얼마 = how much money. 얼마나 = how much (degree).

맛있어요.note: 맛있다 = 'taste exists'. 맛없다 = 'taste doesn't exist' = tasteless. LEGO compound.

맛없어요.note: Don't say this about food someone cooked for you! Try: 맛이 좀 특이해요 (the taste is unique).

uses.e: I will eat well. (said before eating)

잘.note: 잘 먹겠습니다 / 잘 먹었습니다 = the two most important meal phrases in Korean.

uses.e: Please give me (a bit more polite).

좀.note: 좀 softens ANY request and makes you sound more Korean and natural. Use it constantly.

더.note: 더 = more (comparison). 더 이상 = no more (used with negative: 더 이상 없어요).

uses.e: Excuse me, where is the bathroom?

저기요.note: 저기요 = 'hey over there!' Used to call restaurant staff, strangers, shopkeepers. Universal.

주문하다.note: 주문 = Sino-Korean: 주(注/pour) + 문(文/writing). The act of writing/noting an order.

계산.morph: 계산(計算) — Sino-Korean: 계(計/count) + 산(算/calculate)
계산.note: In Korea, you often pay at the counter, not tableside. Say 계산해 주세요 and walk to the register.

포장.morph: 포장(包裝) — Sino-Korean: 포(包/wrap) + 장(裝/pack)

uses.e: Can you pack it to go?

포장.note: 포장 = packaging. Say 포장이요 = to go. 먹고 갈게요 = eating here.

버스.note: 타다 = to ride/board (타요). Use with all transport: 버스를 타다, 택시를 타다, 지하철을 타다.

지하철.note: 지하 = underground, 철 = iron/rail. Seoul subway is one of the world's best — cheap, fast, clean.

uses.e: Is it okay to take a taxi?

택시.note: KakaoTaxi app is the main taxi hailing app in Korea. Cheaper than many countries.

uses.e: Let's meet in front of the station.

역.note: 역 + 에서 = at the station. Korean subway system: 1호선 (Line 1) through 9호선 (Line 9) in Seoul.

uses.e: Let's meet at Hongdae Station exit 9.

출구.note: Korean subway: every station has numbered exits. Meetings are ALWAYS given by exit number, not 'near the station'.

타다.note: 타다 = to ride/board. Works for all transport and vehicles. Opposite: 내리다 (to get off).

엄마.note: 엄마 = casual. 어머니 = formal/respectful. 우리 엄마 (not 나의 엄마) is natural Korean.

아빠.note: 아빠 = casual. 아버지 = formal. The ㅃ (tense b) sounds clipped/forceful — it's not just ㅂ.

어머니.note: Use 어머니 when talking ABOUT others' mothers or in formal settings. Use 엄마 for your own mom casually.

아버지.note: More formal than 아빠. Use when talking about someone else's father or in respectful contexts.

uses.e: Bro, I'm hungry. (male to older brother)
My older brother is a university student.

형.note: ONLY males use 형. Females call older brothers 오빠. Also used for close older male friends.

오빠.note: ONLY females use 오빠. Famous in K-pop: female fans call male idols 오빠. Carries warmth/closeness.

uses.e: My older sister is a chef.

누나.note: ONLY males use 누나. Same grid: Male speaker: 형 (older bro) / 누나 (older sis).

uses.e: My older sister is a doctor.

언니.note: ONLY females use 언니. Also used for older female friends, colleagues. Builds 정 (deep bond).

uses.e: My younger brother is in middle school.
Give it to your younger brother.

남동생.note: 동생 = younger sibling (gender-neutral). 남동생 = male younger sibling. 여동생 = female.

uses.e: My younger sister is in elementary school.
I don't have a younger sister.

여동생.note: 동생 alone = younger sibling (any gender). 여동생 specifically = younger female sibling.

크다.note: 으-irregular: 크다 → 커요. ㅡ drops before 아/어. Contrast with 작다 (small).

작다.note: Regular. 작 + 아요 → 작아요. 큰 ↔ 작은 (modifier forms).

많다.base: to be many / a lot (dictionary form)
많다.note: 많이 (adverb) = a lot. 많다 ≠ 많이. 많 + 아요 → 많아요.

비싸다.note: 비싸 + 아요 → 비싸요. Regular. Opposite: 싸다 (cheap).

싸다.note: Also means 'to wrap/pack' (different word, same spelling). Context distinguishes them.

재미있어요.note: 재미있다 ↔ 재미없다 (boring). Same LEGO logic as 맛있다 ↔ 맛없다.

어렵다.note: ⚡ ㅂ-irregular: 어렵 + 어요 → 어려워요. ㅂ→우 before vowel. Taught fully Unit 7.

쉽다.note: ⚡ ㅂ-irregular like 어렵다. 쉽 + 어요 → 쉬워요. Opposite of 어렵다.

덥다.note: ⚡ ㅂ-irregular: 덥 + 어요 → 더워요. NOT 덥어요. Full pattern Unit 7.

춥다.note: ⚡ ㅂ-irregular like 덥다. 춥 + 어요 → 추워요. Stem 춥 → 추워.

예쁘다.note: 으-irregular: 예쁘 + 어요 → 예뻐요. ㅡ drops. 예쁜 = modifier form.

맵다.note: ⚡ ㅂ-irregular: 맵 + 어요 → 매워요. One of the most useful food adjectives.

달다.note: 달다 is a ㄹ-irregular verb in some forms but 달아요 is the standard polite form.

짜다.note: 짜다 also means 'to squeeze/wring'. Context distinguishes. In food context always = salty.

시.note: Hours use NATIVE Korean numbers: 한 시, 두 시, 세 시... (not 일 시, 이 시). Mix with Sino for minutes.

분.note: Minutes use SINO-KOREAN numbers: 십 분, 이십 분, 삼십 분. 반 = half: 세 시 반 = 3:30.

개.note: 개 = the most versatile counter. When unsure which counter to use, 개 is usually safe.

명.note: 명 = plain counter for people. 분 = polite/honorific version (두 분이세요? in restaurants).

잔.note: 커피 한 잔 주세요 = THE coffee order phrase in Korea. 잔 = cups, glasses, drinks.

원.note: 만 (10,000) is the basic mental unit for Korean money. Think in 만, not in 천.

uses.e: It's the first door on the left.

왼쪽.note: 쪽 = side/direction. Reusable suffix: 앞쪽 (front), 뒤쪽 (back), 이쪽 (this way).

uses.e: It's at the far right end.

오른쪽.note: 왼쪽 ↔ 오른쪽. The 쪽 suffix appears in: 앞쪽, 뒤쪽, 이쪽, 저쪽, 그쪽.

uses.e: Let's meet in front of the school.

앞.note: 앞 ↔ 뒤 (back). 앞쪽 = front side. 앞으로 = forward (direction).

뒤.note: 앞 ↔ 뒤. 뒤쪽 = back side. Can mean 'behind', 'in back of', 'after' in some contexts.

uses.e: It's on top of the desk.

위.note: 위 ↔ 아래 (below). 위에 있어요 = is on top of / above.

아래.note: 위 ↔ 아래. 밑 is a synonym. 아래 is slightly more formal, 밑 more casual.

밖.note: 안 ↔ 밖. 밖에 = only (in certain phrases): 방법이 없는 것밖에 = there's nothing but...

옆.note: 옆 is one of the most useful position words for giving and getting directions.

uses.e: It's between the bank and the café.
What did you do in between?

사이.note: 사이 has TWO meanings: physical 'between' and social 'relationship'. Context distinguishes.

uses.e: It's raining. So I stay home.
I was late. So I took a taxi.

그래서.note: 그래서 starts the RESULT sentence. Cause → 그래서 → Effect.

uses.e: I'll go. But I might be late.
By the way, what's your name?

그런데.note: 그런데 = but/however (contrast) OR topic shift ('by the way'). Very common in conversation.

그러면.base: then / in that case / if so

uses.e: If it rains, then taxi it is.

그러면.note: 그러면 → 그럼 (casual short form). 그럼 is extremely common in conversation.

아니면.note: 아니면 = the main 'or' connector in Korean questions. More natural than 또는 in speech.

uses.e: I was late. But I came.

하지만.note: 하지만 = more formal than 그런데. Both mean 'but'. 그런데 is more common in speech.

자주.note: Frequency scale: 항상 (always) > 자주 (often) > 가끔 (sometimes) > 별로 (not really) > 전혀 (never).

가끔.note: Frequency: 자주 (often) > 가끔 (sometimes) > 별로 안 (not really).

보통.note: 보통 = usually (frequency) AND ordinary/average (adjective). Context tells which.

별로.note: 별로 ALWAYS pairs with a negative. 별로 좋아요 ✗. 별로 안 좋아요 ✓.

전혀.note: 전혀 = absolute zero. Always with negative. Stronger than 별로. 전혀 없어요 = absolutely none.

안녕하세요.note: The universal Korean greeting for all adults and strangers. 안녕 alone = casual (friends only).

감사합니다.note: Most formal 'thank you'. 고맙습니다 is warmer/less stiff. Both take 정말 (really) as intensifier.

고맙습니다.note: 고맙습니다 is slightly warmer and less stiff than 감사합니다. Both are polite.

괜찮아요.base: it's okay / are you okay / no thanks
괜찮아요.note: 괜찮아요 has 3 uses: 1) I'm fine 2) Are you okay? 3) Politely declining. Context is everything.

죄송합니다.note: 죄송합니다 = formal deep apology. 미안해요 = casual sorry. 실례합니다 = 'excuse me' (passing by).

uses.e: Meeting you for the first time. (super formal)

만나서반갑습니다.note: 만나서 = 'having met'. 반갑다 = glad/pleased. Bow slightly when you say this. Essential first meeting phrase.

잘부탁드립니다.base: I look forward to working with you

uses.e: Please take care of me. / Looking forward to working with you.
I look forward to our future relationship.
(Casual version) Please take good care of me.

잘부탁드립니다.note: Untranslatable but critical phrase. Said when joining a team, meeting business partners, or starting any new relationship.

가다.note: Dictionary form. Drop 다 → stem 가. Add 요 → 가요. Train metaphor: 가 is the engine.

오다.note: Dictionary form of 와요. 오 + 아요 → 와요 (vowel contraction). Stem: 오.

먹다.note: Dictionary form. 먹 + 어요 → 먹어요. Stem ends in dark vowel ㅓ → takes -어요.

마시다.note: 마시 + 어요 → 마셔요. 이+어 → 여 contraction. Dictionary form shown here.

하다.note: Most frequent Korean verb. 하다 + 어요 contracts to 해요. Powers all [noun]+하다 verbs.

보다.note: 보 + 아요 → 봐요. Pure vowel stem. Also means 'to try' in -아/어 보다 form.

읽다.note: 읽 + 어요 → 읽어요. The ㄱ in 읽 is pronounced only before a vowel.

쓰다.note: 쓰 + 어요 → 써요. ㅡ-irregular: ㅡ drops before 아/어. Also means 'to wear (hat/glasses)'.

알다.note: ㄹ-irregular in some forms. 알아요 is regular. Pair with 모르다 (don't know).

모르다.note: ⚡ JRTF: 모르다 → 몰라요 (ㄹ-irregular). Drop ㄹ before ㅏ/ㅗ and double: 몰+라요.

주다.note: 주다 → 주세요 = 'please give'. 주 + 어요 → 줘요. Pairs with 받다 (to receive).

좋다.base: to be good / to like (dictionary form)

uses.e: This is good / I like this.

좋다.note: 좋다 = BOTH 'to be good' AND 'to like'. 좋 + 아요 → 좋아요.

싫다.note: 싫 + 어요 → 싫어요. More direct/emotional than 안 좋아요.

의사.note: 사(師) = expert in a field. 의사, 요리사, 간호사 all share this 사 morpheme.

선생.note: 선생 alone is informal. Always add 님 in actual use: 선생님. 님 = respect honorific suffix.

uses.e: I want to become an office worker.

회사원.note: 원(員) = member/person. 회사원, 공무원, 병원 all use 원 (though 병원의 원 = place, not person).

카페.note: Korea has one of the world's highest café densities. 카페 culture = 공부, 미팅, 데이트.

병원.note: 병원 = any medical facility. 종합병원 = general hospital. 의원 = small clinic.

uses.e: There are many books in the library.

도서관.note: 관(館) = public building. 관 reappears in: 미술관 (art museum), 박물관 (museum), 체육관 (gym).
